segments.s587-g65: نه at the start negates the whole clause. این بود is a copular phrase ('it was so'); چشم امیدم is a possessive noun phrase ('the eye of my hope' = 'my hope/expectation'), with the pronominal suffix -م marking 'my'. به شاه is a prepositional phrase showing the target/destination of that hope. The overall order is topic (negation + copula) then predicate phrase (possession + prepositional target).
segments.s587-g66: که introduces a relative/content clause 'that ...'. زین سان is an idiom 'in this way/thus' (زین = 'this' archaic + سان = 'like/as'). کند is the classical narrative/present-form verb here used to express the action ('he would/do/does turn'); سوی marks direction 'toward', کهتر modifies the noun (a lesser/inferior person) and نگاه is the noun 'gaze/look' which is the object of کند. Word order is relative clause with verb toward the end of the clause.
segments.s587-g67: The repeated نه ... نه pattern lists negatives ('neither ... nor ...'). فرزندم is 'my son' (noun + pronominal suffix -م). ایدر is an archaic adverb meaning 'here' giving location. چون is a comparative/relational particle 'like/as' introducing the next item; چاکری here means servitude/service, i.e. 'a servant' or 'servancy' — the clause denies the presence or likeness of such a person.
segments.s587-g68: چون again introduces a comparison/list item 'nor as/none like'. کهتری here means 'one of lesser rank' (a lowly person). شاددل is an adjective 'cheerful, glad‑hearted' describing that person. بر is the locative/prepositional element 'on/at/upon' and دری (here) would mark the place ('at the gate/court' in context), although دری's listed senses don't match this exact use (see senses). The phrase denies that any lowly, glad‑hearted person is present at that place.
segments.s587-g69: به + proper name marks the indirect object 'to Nu'mān'. بگفت is the past-tense verb 'said/told' (classical past form). آنچ is a relative/complement meaning 'that which/what'. بودش is the past copula بود with the enclitic -ش ('it/that') — here indicating 'that which had been' — and نهان is the adjective/participle 'hidden'. Together they form 'he told Nu'mān that which had been hidden.'
segments.s587-g70: ز is the poetic/prepositional form 'from/because of' (introducing source or cause). بد راه و آیین is a compound meaning 'bad ways and customs' (بد qualifies راه 'way' and آیین 'custom/practice'). شاه جهان is a genitive phrase 'the king of the world' (شاه governing جهان). The whole prepositional phrase specifies the domain of what was hidden: concerning the king's bad conduct and practices.
segments.s587-g71: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. نعمان is the subject proper name. برفت is an archaic/perfective past form meaning 'went forth/left' (classical narrative past), here marking the action of leaving. از در is a prepositional phrase 'from the gate/door' (از = from; در = gate/door), and شهریار is the genitival modifier 'of the king' (مضاف/مضاف‌الیه).
segments.s587-g72: بیامد is the past tense 'he came'. بر here is a preposition meaning 'to/toward' (used with a person/place). منذر is the personal name 'Munḏir' as the destination, and نامدار is an adjective 'renowned/famed' qualifying him. The short clause thus reports the arrival at Munḏir.
segments.s587-g105: هرانگه که is a temporal subordinate introducer meaning 'whenever/when'. دینار بردی is a past/simple perfect second-person singular (بردی = you took/received) with دینار as its object; به کار is a prepositional phrase 'for the work' (به = for/to, کار = work/service) that qualifies the taking. Word order is typical Persian: temporal clause + subject/verb phrase, and the relative/temporal که links this clause to what follows.
segments.s587-g106: گرانی مکن is an imperative negative: مکن ('do not') + گرانی (a noun meaning literal 'heaviness' used figuratively as 'being weighty/arrogant'); هیچ here functions as an emphatic negation 'at all'. بر شهریار uses the preposition بر (toward/against) with شهریار 'the king' as the target. Overall: 'do not act weighty/arrogant at all toward the king.'
segments.s587-g107: که begins a clause giving a reason/explanation ('for'/'that'); دیگر modifies فرستمت to mean 'other/more' or 'again', فرستمت is a1st-person preterite 'I sent you' (the -ت marks the object), بسیار 'many/much' intensifies, and نیز 'also/too' adds inclusively. Word order places the verb with attached object before modifiers, typical of older Persian.
segments.s587-g108: وزین پادشاهی is a nominal phrase where وزین (literally 'weighty' or 'dignified') qualifies پادشاهی 'kingship/royalty'; ز introduces the source/contents ('from') and هرگونه چیز 'every kind of thing' is a genitive-like phrase enumerating what was sent. The clause is elliptical (no new verb) and completes the explanation begun by که.
segments.s587-g109: پرستنده باش و ستاینده باش uses two imperatives: باش 'be' applied to participial nouns پرستنده ('one who worships/devotes') and ستاینده ('one who praises'). The conjunction و links the two imperatives as parallel commands; the construction is a common hortative in classical Persian.
segments.s587-g110: به کار پرستش فزاینده باش is another imperative clause instructing manner: به کار + noun phrase (کار پرستش = 'the work/act of worship') and فزاینده 'increasing' modifies باش to mean 'be increasing' or 'be ever-growing' in that work. The adjective فزاینده is predicated of the subject via the imperative verb باش.
segments.s587-g111: تو is the emphatic second-person subject 'you'; آن خوی بد را is a definite object marked by را ('that bad disposition'); ز شاه جهان uses the poetic preposition ز 'from' with شاه جهان 'king of the world' (a title). The phrase sets up what is to be separated (the object) and from whom (the king).
segments.s587-g112: جدا کرد نتوانی is a verbal complex: جدا (apart/separately) + کرد (causative/do/make) + نتوانی (you cannot); together they mean 'you cannot make X separate' = 'you cannot separate X'. اندر نهان is a locative/adiunct 'in secret/hiddenly' (اندر = in/within, نهان = hidden). Word order places the negated ability after the verb-stem, as in classical Persian.
segments.s587-g1: پدر (subject) + آرزو (noun 'wish') + کرد (past-tense verb 'did/made') form a transitive clause: 'the father made a wish (for) Bahram'. The following را is the Persian definite/object marker attached to بهرام, marking Bahram as the direct object of کرد. Word order is somewhat topicalized (verb before subject) because this is poetic.
segments.s587-g2: چه is an exclamative particle here ('what' / 'what a…'). The sequence بهرام خورشید خودکام را is an appositive/epithet: بهرام (name) described as خورشید ('sun' / shining luminary) + خودکام (adjective 'autocratic, arbitrary'). The final را again marks the phrase as definite/emphatic. The whole segment is an emphatic exclamation applied to Bahram.
segments.s587-g3: به + منذر is the prepositional object 'to Munḏir'; چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. In this poetic order the verb precedes the subject: 'to Munḏir thus said Bahram the Lion' (i.e., 'Bahram the Lion thus said to Munḏir').
segments.s587-g4: که introduces the reported/subordinate clause ('that' / here functioning like 'though'). هرچند is a concessive particle 'although'; مانیم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive 'we remain' (suffix -یم marks 'we'); نزد تو is a prepositional phrase 'in/near you / in your presence' (نزد + pronoun تو); دیر modifies duration 'long' (i.e., 'we may linger long'). Together: 'that although we may remain long in your presence.'
segments.s587-g5: همان آرزوی پدر is a genitive phrase 'that same wish of (my) father' (همان = 'that very'); خیزدم is the 1st‑person past form of خیزدن 'I rose / I advanced'. Together: 'I rose for/at that same father's wish.'
segments.s587-g6: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; ایمن 'safe/secure' is predicate; شوم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I may become / I become', used here as a temporal subordinate ('when I become secure'); در is the preposition/prefix 'in' (here functioning with the verb); برانگیزدم is 1st‑person verb 'I aroused / I will stir up' (classically formed). The clause means 'when I am secure, I will rouse (them)', with شوم + verb expressing the temporal/conditional sequence.
segments.s587-g7: برآرست is a 3rd‑person past verb 'he set up / he prepared / he set out'; since this is poetry the verb appears before the subject: 'Munḏir set out'. چو introduces the temporal clause 'when'; بایست here expresses necessity/obligation ('it was necessary' / 'the time came that it must be done'); کار is the noun 'work/task'. Together: 'Munḏir set out when the work required it.'
segments.s587-g8: ز (poetic form of از) = 'from'; شهر یمن = 'the city of Yemen' (شهر governor/name + یمن specification); هدیه شهریار is a noun phrase 'a gift/tribute for the king' or 'the king's gift' (شهریار = king). The whole short noun phrase supplies what was set out: 'a royal gift from the city of Yemen.'
segments.s587-g41: بدین کار is a prepositional phrase meaning 'by/for this deed' (بدین = به + این). پاداش is the noun 'reward' and نزد منست is a verbless or copular clause where نزد 'with/at (the side of)' governs the following predicate; منست is an archaic/poetic copular expression (functioning like 'is (to) me' or marking the predicate) — the whole segment reads 'For this deed the reward is with me.'
segments.s587-g42: This is a short nominal clause: بهار شما 'your spring' (بهار used metaphorically for prosperity/fortune) followed by اورمزد (Ahura Mazda) as the possessor/source and the same archaic predicate منست; together it states that the addressee's spring/prosperity is (from/belongs to) Ahura Mazda.
segments.s587-g43: پسندیدم is a simple past/perfect first‑person verb 'I approved/liked.' این رای و فرهنگ اوی is the direct object: 'this counsel/opinion and his manner/culture' (اوی here is a genitive/possessive form 'his'). The coordination with و links رای and فرهنگ as two objects approved by the speaker.
segments.s587-g44: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that / for' giving the reason; سوی خرد is a prepositional phrase 'toward wisdom' (سوی = toward, خرد = wisdom), بینم is first‑person present 'I see' or 'I perceive,' and آهنگ اوی ('his intention/aim') is the object of perception. Word order is subject (implicit 'I' in بینم) + verb + object (آهنگ اوی), with the directional phrase سوی خرد qualifying the object.
segments.s587-g45: تو is the vocative/subject 'you.' چون here has a causal/temporal sense 'since/as,' دیر ماندی is a past tense clause 'you stayed/lingered late,' and بدین بارگاه is a locative 'in/at this court' (بدین = به + این). The phrase orders cause/temporal marker + verb + locative complement.
segments.s587-g46: پدر چشم دارد is a simple clause 'father keeps an eye / watches'; دارد is the present tense auxiliary/verb 'has/keeps.' همانا is an emphatic adverb 'indeed/verily,' and به راه is a prepositional phrase 'on the road/way' indicating the object/direction of his watchfulness.
segments.s587-g47: ز دینار گنجیش is a prepositional phrase 'from the dinars of the treasury' (ز = from, دینار = dinar/money, گنجیش = treasury/vault). پنجه هزار is an archaic numeric expression meaning 'five thousand' (word order places the numeral after the quantified noun), so the whole segment supplies the source and amount of the gift.
segments.s587-g48: بدادند is the past tense verb 'they gave/granted.' با جامه شهریار is an accompanying phrase 'with the king's garment/robe' (با = with, جامه = robe, شهریار = king), indicating that the robe accompanied the monetary gift.
segments.s587-g9: This is a short prepositional phrase headed by the poetic preposition ز (z, “from”), followed by the plural noun اسپان (aspān, “steeds/horses”) modified by the adjective تازی (tāzī, “Arabian”). به introduces the other endpoint/limit of the range (’to’), and زرین is the adjective “golden” modifying ستام. The whole phrase lists an extent or range of booty/items (’from X to Y’).
segments.s587-g10: Another clause beginning with the poetic preposition ز (’from’). چیزی که is a relative clause introducer (’that/which’). پرمایه here qualifies the following object as valuable/substantial, and بردند is a past tense plural verb (they took/won); نام is the object meaning reputation/fame. Together this says that from those valuable things they carried off, they obtained a name/reputation.
segments.s587-g11: Starts with ز (’from’) marking items taken: برد here denotes booty/gain and is modified by یمانی (’Yemeni’), followed by و (’and’) and تیغ یمن (’the sword of Yemen’). The sequence enumerates types of spoils associated with Yemen.
segments.s587-g12: دگر هرچ is an idiomatic phrase meaning ’whatever else’ or ’any other thing’; معدنش is معدن (mine/source) with possessive -ش (’its/mine of it’), followed by the predicate بد (bad/poor) and the locative در عدن (’in Aden’). The clause says that any other ores/mines it may have are of poor quality in Aden.
segments.s587-g13: چو here functions as a temporal conjunction ’when’. نعمان is the proper name Nuʿmān; که introduces a relative clause modifying him (’who’). با شاه همراه بود is a verbal clause with همراه as noun/adjective (’companion/with’) and بود the past copula (’was’): ’who was with/attending the king’.
segments.s587-g14: به نزدیک او is a locative phrase ’near him’ (به + noun). افسر ماه بود is a nominal predicate: افسر here = ’helmet’ (a common poetic usage), ماه = ’moon’ used adjectivally/figuratively to mean shining or beautiful, and بود is the past copula (’was’). The phrase thus describes a gleaming/’moon-like’ helmet being beside him.
segments.s587-g15: چنین تا sets the manner and extent: ’thus/until’. تا + به شهر ... آمدند marks the endpoint ’until they came to the city …’. شهر صطخر names the destination (Istakhr) and آمدند is the past plural verb ’they came/proceeded’.
segments.s587-g16: که here introduces a relative/explanatory clause attached to آمدند: از شاه‌زاد (’from the prince’) is an ablative phrase indicating origin or association, به فخر آمدند is an idiom meaning ’they came with pride/boast’. آمدند is again the past plural verb. The clause explains that their arrival was in proud relation to the prince.
segments.s587-g97: فرستم is a first-person verb 'I send / I will send' (classical present/future nuance). نگر is an imperative interjection 'look / behold' that directs the addressee to attend. دل نداری is a nominal phrase meaning 'you (or one) lack a heart/courage' (دل = 'heart' used for courage/feeling; نداری = 'lack'), and به رنج attaches with به as 'for/to' indicating what the heart lacks — 'a heart for hardship.' The phrase order is poetic (verb/interjection first, then the descriptive clause).
segments.s587-g98: نیرزد is a stative verb 'is not worth / does not merit' and here governs the rest of the clause. پراگنده رنج تو is a noun phrase 'your scattered toil/sufferings' (پراگنده = 'scattered', رنج = 'suffering/toil', تو = 'your'), and گنج is the noun 'treasure' serving as the standard of comparison; poetic word order places the verb first: 'Your scattered toil is not worth a treasure.'
segments.s587-g99: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' or 'of' introducing source/material. دینار names the coin (dinar). گنجی is an indefinite/qualifying form 'a treasure' (‑ی marks indefinite/relational), and کنون 'now' + ده هزار 'ten thousand' give the amount: 'a treasure (amounting) now ten thousand (dinars).' Word order is inverted for poetic effect: source → item → amount.
segments.s587-g100: فرستادم is past tense 'I sent / I have sent' (first person). اینک 'now' emphasizes the timing. ز بهر is a compound prepositional idea 'for the sake of / for', and نثار is 'offering / gift'; together فرستادم اینک ز بهر نثار = 'I have (now) sent (it) as an offering.'
segments.s587-g101: پرستار is 'attendant / caretaker'. کو here is an interrogative particle 'where is / where are' (classical poetic usage introducing a rhetorical question). رهنمای تو 'your guide' is a descriptive apposition, and بود is the past copula 'was'. The line asks rhetorically 'Where is the attendant who was your guide?'
segments.s587-g102: به پرده درون is a locative phrase 'inside the veil / behind the curtain' (به + noun + درون = 'within'). دلگشای تو is a compound epithet 'your heart-opener / your comforter' (دل + گشای), and بود again is the past copula 'was' placed at the end: 'Within the curtain was your heart-opener.' The line uses compact, poetic word order: locative phrase → epithet → copula.
segments.s587-g103: فرستادم is past 'I sent / I have sent.' اینک 'now' timestamps the action. به نزدیک تو is a prepositional phrase 'to (or near) your vicinity' (به = 'to/for', نزدیک = 'near', تو = 'you/your'), indicating the destination of what was sent. The clause is a simple send + destination construction.
segments.s587-g104: که introduces a purposive/subordinate clause 'so that / in order that.' روشن here is used adjectivally/verbal in the sense 'make bright / enlighten' and is combined with کند (3rd-singular present/subjunctive 'may do / may make') to form 'may make bright / enlighten.' جان تاریک تو is 'your dark soul' (جان = 'soul/life', تاریک = 'dark', تو = 'your'). The whole clause = 'so that he may brighten/enlighten your dark soul.'
segments.s587-g89: Literal order is poetic: 'سپهر' (the heavens) is the agent/subject, 'روان را' (about/with respect to the soul) is marked by the object marker را, and the clause closes with 'چنین است رای' — 'such is (its) opinion.' چنان/چنین + است here function as a copular predicate describing رای ('opinion/counsel').
segments.s587-g90: This is an elliptical verbless clause: 'تو با رای او' ('you, together with his opinion/counsel') forms a prepositional/agent phrase, followed by the nominal 'هیچ مفزای پای' ('no refuge/firm footing'). The negative modifier 'هیچ' negates 'مفزای' (refuge), and 'پای' is used metaphorically as 'footing' or 'secure base.'
segments.s587-g91: Poetic word order: 'دلی را' ('a heart' or 'one heart', marked by را) is the object, followed by the predicate 'پر از مهر دارد سپهر' — 'the heavens has (or keeps) [it] full of love.' 'پر از' is the idiom 'full of', and 'دارد' is the present/past possession verb placed before the subject 'سپهر' for metrical/poetic effect.
segments.s587-g92: This is a parallel descriptive phrase: 'دلی پر ز کین' = 'a heart full of hatred' (پر + ز = 'full of'), coordinated with 'و پر آژنگ چهر' = 'and a face full of āzhang' (آژنگ is an archaic/poetic word used metaphorically here). There is no overt verb; the two noun phrases are coordinated and understood as examples of what the heavens bestows or contains.
segments.s587-g93: Straightforward past-tense clause: 'جهاندار' (the world-ruler) is the agent, 'گیتی' (the world) is the object, 'چنین' is an adverb 'thus,' and 'آفرید' is the past verb 'created.' Word order (agent–object–adverb–verb) is poetically marked but grammatically regular for older Persian.
segments.s587-g94: This clause is compressed/elliptical and somewhat archaic: 'چنان' = 'such/so,' 'کو' functions as a relative/archaising particle here ('that/which'), 'چماند' = 'remained, was left,' and the modal 'بباید' = 'must/ought to' takes the following action 'چمید' — an old verb/noun-form meaning to bolt/fasten. Together: 'That which so remained must be fastened.'
segments.s587-g95: 'ازین پس' = 'from now on' (poetic contraction of 'از این پس'); 'ترا' = 'to you' (object/beneficiary); 'هرچ آید به کار' = 'whatever comes to use / whatever is of use' — 'هرچ' = 'whatever,' 'آید' = 'comes/happens,' and 'به کار' = 'to (be) of use.' The whole phrase sets up a list of useful things that follow.
segments.s587-g96: A terse enumeration introduced by poetic prepositional 'ز' = 'from/of': 'ز دینار' = 'from dinars (money),' then 'وز' is a fused poetic form (و + ز) meaning roughly 'and from' (not the literal senses listed), followed by 'گوهر' = 'gems' and 'شاهوار' = 'regal/lofty' modifying it. The line lists types of wealth that might be of use.
segments.s587-g33: شب و روز is a temporal adverbial phrase ‘night and day’ (continuous, intensive time). بهرام is the subject (proper name). پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase meaning ‘in the presence of / before his father’ (پیش + noun); word order is typical Persian: time phrase — subject — prepositional complement.
segments.s587-g34: همی is the frequentive/continuous particle meaning ‘always/continually’. از + noun (از پرستش) marks the source/thing involved here: ‘from / with worship’. نخارید is a poetic/archaic negated verbal form; in this line it functions as the main verb meaning ‘did not lift/raise’ (i.e. did not raise his head) — the negation is fused into the verb in older Shahnameh usage. سر is the direct object ‘head’ and completes the idiom ‘سر را نیاورد/سر را نخَست’ (did not lift the head).
segments.s587-g35: چو here = ‘when’ (a subordinating conjunction). ماه is used poetically (‘the moon’ → ‘a beautiful/bright one’) to describe نعمان (a proper name: Nu'mān) — the phrase likens Nu'mān to a moon. ببد is an older/poetic past form meaning ‘became / appeared / was’ (third-person past). نزد شاه = ‘in the presence of the king’ (نزد = near/in the presence of; شاه = king).
segments.s587-g36: همی again marks continuous or habitual aspect. خواست is the past form of خواستن ‘wanted/wished’. تا introduces a purpose/subordinate clause here (‘that / so that’) and governs the following subjunctive-like verb. بازگردد is the infinitive/3rd-person present-subjunctive form of بازگشتن ‘to return’ (meaning ‘he might return’). به راه = ‘to the road / back on the path’ (به = to/toward; راه = way/path).
segments.s587-g37: بشب is an adverbial phrase 'by night / at night' placed before the verb. کس is an indefinite noun 'someone' and is the direct object of فرستاد, which is a past-tense verb 'sent'. The conjunction و links two coordinated actions; او را is the clitic object with the accusative marker را, and بخواند (here a classical/poetic form of خواندن with the prefixed b-) means 'to call/summon' (i.e. 'had him summoned').
segments.s587-g38: برابرش is برابر + ـش (the clitic = 'in front of him / opposite him'), where the clitic refers back to the summoned person. بر تخت شاهی is a prepositional phrase 'on the royal throne' (بر + تخت; شاهی is an adjective 'royal'). نشاند is the archaic past/causative meaning 'caused to sit / seated (him)' — together the phrase means 'he seated him on the royal throne opposite him.'
segments.s587-g39: بدو is a dative/enclitic phrase 'to him' (marks the indirect object/recipient). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' introducing reported speech; the name منذر follows as the speaker (classical word order allows the name after گفت). بسی is an adverb 'very / much' modifying رنج, and رنج دید is an idiomatic combination where دید (literally 'saw') functions as a light verb to mean 'suffered / experienced suffering' (past tense).
segments.s587-g40: که introduces the subordinate/content clause ('that / because') giving the reason for the previous statement. آزاده is an epithet/adjective 'noble-hearted / noble' modifying the name بهرام; بهرام را is the definite object (را marks the object). پرورید is the past of پروردن 'to nurture/raise' and completes the clause: '(that) I raised the noble Bahram.' The subordinate clause explains the cause of the suffering mentioned in the previous segment.
segments.s587-g25: شگفتی is the noun 'amazement/marvel' placed before the verb; فروماند is a past-tense verbal form meaning 'remained/was left' (classical word order here: Subject Noun + Verb). از introduces the cause or focus ('at/because of') and governs کار اوی, where کار is 'deed' and اوی is the archaic genitive/enclitic meaning 'his'.
segments.s587-g26: ز (poetic variant of از) functions like 'because of/at' and takes a list of qualities: بالا 'loftiness/high rank' and فرهنگ 'refinement/culture' are coordinated by و. دیدار here refers to the way he was seen / his appearance; اوی is the same archaic possessive 'his'. The entire phrase continues the causa/target of 'شگفتی فروماند'.
segments.s587-g27: فراوان is an adverbial qualifier 'frequently/abundantly' modifying the verb بپرسید; بپرسید here is a past/perfective inquiry verb 'he asked' (classical forme with initial ب-). و links the next action بنواختش, where نواختن is used in the past tense and the enclitic -ش ('him') marks the object — 'he played/entertained him'.
segments.s587-g28: به نزدیک خود is a prepositional phrase 'to/at near himself' (به + نزدیک + reflexive خود). جایگه is 'place/seat/position' and ساختش is past 'he made/built' with enclitic -ش referring back to the person (i.e. 'he gave/made for him a place'). Word order is typical of classical Persian: prepositional phrase + object + verb.
segments.s587-g29: به introduces the beneficiary/goal ('for'); برزن درون is a compound locative 'the inner plaza/square' (درون = 'inside'); جای نعمان is a genitive construction 'Nu'mān's place' meaning 'a place for Nu'mān'; گزید is the past tense 'he chose/selected'. The order places the locative before the possessed noun.
segments.s587-g30: یکی is 'one/a certain' introducing a single item; کاخ بهرام is a genitive 'Bahram's palace(s)'; را is the definite object marker marking کاخ بهرام as the object; چون here means 'as/just as' and سزید is the past of سزایدن used impersonally 'it befitted' or 'as was fitting' (i.e. he allotted one palace as was proper).
segments.s587-g31: فرستاد is the simple past 'he sent/dispatch(ed)'; نزدیک او is a locative phrase 'near him' (نزدیک + pronoun او); بندگان is the direct object 'servants' (note the verb-front order, common in poetry: Verb + adverbial + object).
segments.s587-g32: چو is the simile 'as/like'; اندر means 'in/within'; خور here appears as a court/household term (classical usage 'the royal table/house'), so چو اندر خور او forms a simile 'as (if) within his household'; پرستندگان is the simile's complement 'worshippers/devotees' — together: 'like worshippers within his court'.
segments.s587-g49: ز آخر is a poetic adverbial phrase meaning “from the end,” i.e. “at last” or “finally.” The prepositional phrase به سیمین و زرین لگام uses به + adjectives + noun to indicate accompaniment or instrumentation (here idiomatically “with” silver and golden bridles); سیمین and زرین are adjectival (silver/golden) placed before the head لگام for emphasis, and و simply links the two adjectives.
segments.s587-g50: ده اسپ گرانمایه is a noun phrase with the numeral ده before the noun اسپ and the adjective گرانمایه (‘precious’) qualifying it; Persian allows adjective either before or after the noun in classical/poetic style. بردند is the 3rd-person plural past of بردن (“they took/carried”); here it is used to report that they took/removed/led away the horses. نام at the end is used in the sense “renown/fame” (idiomatically بردنِ نام = to gain a name/fame) so together the clause says they took the steeds and gained renown.
segments.s587-g51: ز here begins a listing phrase meaning “from/of”; گستردنیهای is a plural noun built from the verbal base گستردن + -نی (nominalizer) + plural suffix, meaning “things to be spread” (caparisons, coverings, trappings). زیبنده is an adjective (‘befitting, comely’) modifying that plural; نیز follows to mean “also / likewise,” marking this as an additional category in the catalogue.
segments.s587-g52: This is a rhythmic list formed by repeating ز before each item: ز رنگ و ز بوی و ز هرگونه چیز = “from color and from scent and from every kind of thing.” رنگ and بوی are simple nouns (“color,” “scent”), هرگونه is a compound meaning “any kind,” and چیز is the generic noun “thing.” The repeated ز adds distributive/emphatic force.
segments.s587-g53: ز گنج جهاندار ایران is a genitive/partitive phrase: ز = “from,” گنج = “treasure,” and جهاندار ایران = “the world‑ruler of Iran” (a compound/title modifying گنج: “the treasure of the sovereign of Iran”). ببرد is a 3rd‑person singular verbal form of بردن used in classical diction here to mean “he took / carried off.”
segments.s587-g54: یکایک is an adverb meaning “one by one / each in turn.” به introduces the indirect object “to” (به نعمان منذر = to Nuʿmān, Munḏir); note the two proper names are listed in sequence. سپرد is the past 3rd‑person singular of سپردن, “he entrusted / handed over,” completing the action begun in the previous clause.
segments.s587-g55: به شادی در بخشش is a stacked adverbial phrase: به شادی = “in joy,” در بخشش = “in (the) giving / generosity.” اندر گشاد is a compact poetic clause where گشاد (‘wide/open’) describes the action of opening (the verb ‘to open’ is implied); together it conveys “he opened wide (his hand) in giving.”
segments.s587-g56: بر اندازه یارانش را هدیه داد is a full verb clause: بر اندازه = “according to the measure/degree,” یارانش = “his companions” (یاران + possessive -ش), را is the definite/object marker, هدیه = “a gift,” and داد is the past 3rd‑person singular of دادن (“he gave”). The sequence thus means he gave gifts to his companions in proportion to their rank.
segments.s587-g57: This phrase is a compact poetic clause: به + indirect object (منذر) marks the recipient 'to Munẓir'; یکی نامه = 'a letter' (یکی here indefinite); بنوشت is the past tense 'wrote' in elevated/poetic form; شاه is the logical subject placed after the verb for rhythmic/poetic word order (verb–subject inversion).
segments.s587-g58: چنانچون بود = 'just as/just so was' (چنانچون = 'just as', بود = past 'was'); در خور is best read together as درخور 'befitting/fit for' (در + خور used adjectivally), and پیشگاه = 'the royal presence/court'. The clause means 'as was fitting in the court's presence.'
segments.s587-g59: This noun phrase expresses granting release: به + آزادی marks the giving of 'freedom/release'; از کار = 'from duty/work'; فرزند اوی = 'his son' (اوی is the possessive/enclitic 'his'). In context the sense is 'gave his son release from his duties.'
segments.s587-g60: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for/since' or 'who'): شاه یمن = 'the king of Yemen' (compound noun), گشت = past of شدن 'became', پیوند اوی = 'his bond/ally' (پیوند used as 'ally/link', اوی = 'his'). Together: 'for his son became king of Yemen and his ally.'
segments.s587-g61: به پاداش این کار = 'as a reward for this deed' (به marks purpose/benefit, پاداش = reward, این کار = this deed). یازم is a poetic/archaic verb here meaning roughly 'he gave to me' (not listed in the provided senses) and همی is an emphatic particle. The clause is elliptical in Persian poetry: the giving is expressed compactly.
segments.s587-g62: به چونین پسر = 'such a son' (به marking the recipient/beneficiary in the giving act, چونین = 'such'), سرفرازم = 'my proud/esteemed one' (سرفراز 'proud/victorious' with enclitic -م = 'my'), and همی is emphatic. Word order places descriptive element after the verb cluster in poetic style.
segments.s587-g63: یکی نامه = 'a letter' (یکی indefinite before نامه); بنوشت is the past 'wrote' in poetic diction (verb precedes subject here); بهرام گور is the subject 'Bahram Gur' (گور here is the epithet 'Gūr' = the onager, used as part of his name).
segments.s587-g64: که introduces reported content ('that'): کار من = 'my affairs' (کار = affairs/work, من = my), ایدر = archaic 'here', تباهست = 'is ruined' (past/state; poetic fused form), و شور = 'and (is) in turmoil' (شور = tumult/commotion). The whole clause reports the letter's complaint about ruined affairs here.
segments.s587-g81: هم‌اندر زمان زود is a temporal phrase placed before the verb to set the time: a poetic way of saying ‘at that same moment’ or ‘immediately’. نوشت is a simple past (third-person singular) ‘wrote’; پاسخ is its direct object placed immediately before the verb, following normal Persian word order in poetry. The whole segment means ‘he immediately wrote a reply.’
segments.s587-g82: This is a noun phrase plus verb: سخنهای (plural ‘words/sayings’) qualified by the prepositional phrase با مغز و فرخ (‘with/made of مغز and فرخ’), where مغز is used metaphorically for ‘substance/wit’ and فرخ is an adjectival quality ‘auspicious/felicitous’. The verb نوشت repeats the action; stylistically the second نوشت restates that he ‘wrote such (worthy) words.’
segments.s587-g83: چنین گفت is ‘thus he said’ (چنین = thus). The following phrase کای مهتر نامور is a vocative: کای = “O” addressing someone, مهتر نامور = “renowned elder/chief.” Note: گفت here functions as the reporting verb ‘said’ (poetic usage places the comment after the verb).
segments.s587-g84: نگر is an imperative ‘look!’ or ‘see/consider’; it introduces the admonition. سر نپیچی is a negative second-person imperative formed with the negative prefix ن- plus the present-stem پیچی ‘do not turn/warp your head’; ز راه پدر is a prepositional phrase ‘from/away from the father's way’ giving the direction of the prohibition. The clause as a whole: ‘look — do not deviate from your father's path.’
segments.s587-g85: به نیک و بد is an adverbial phrase ‘in good and bad (times)’ or ‘in both prosperity and adversity’. شاه is the indirect object/target of the adjective خرسند (‘pleased/content with the king’). باش is the imperative verb ‘be’ (second-person). The construction orders the temporal/adverbial phrase before the target and the predicate adjective, giving the command ‘be content toward the king in good and bad.’
segments.s587-g86: Two coordinated imperatives: پرستنده باش (‘be a worshipper/devotee’) and خردمند باش (‘be wise’). پرستنده is a noun used predicatively, followed by the imperative copula باش; the conjunction و links the two parallel commands, and the second باش repeats for emphasis and symmetry — a common poetic device.
segments.s587-g87: بدیها is the plural subject ‘evils/wrongdoings.’ به صبر is an instrumental/adverbial phrase ‘by/with patience’ (به used here to mark the means). از مهان (‘from guests’) is a prepositional phrase showing source, and بگذرد is a third-person singular verb ‘passes/shall pass’ (poetic present/future sense). The clause means roughly ‘bad things are passed over from guests by patience.’
segments.s587-g88: سر مرد is an idiomatic phrase where سر (‘head/honor/essence’) is possessed by مرد (‘a man’). باید is the modal ‘must/ought to,’ followed by a relative clause که دارد خرد (‘who has wisdom’), where دارد is the present-tense ‘has’ and خرد means ‘wisdom.’ Together: ‘A man’s head (i.e. a true man) must have wisdom,’ i.e. ‘a man ought to be wise.’
segments.s587-g73: بدو is the dative clitic/phrase 'to him' (here 'unto him') placed before the noun phrase. 'نامه شاه گیتی' is a genitive chain meaning 'the letter of the king of the world' (noun + possessor). بداد here functions as a past-tense verb 'gave' (3rd sing. past) completing the clause; note the form is poetic/older usage of دادن.
segments.s587-g74: ببوسید is a past (3rd sg.) verb 'he kissed' placed before its subject (poetic inversion). منذر after the verb is the subject 'Munḏir'. 'به سر بر نهاد' is a light-verb construction: به + سر (head) + بر + نهاد (past of نهادن 'placed') = 'placed (it) upon his head'; the object (the letter) is omitted but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s587-g75: وزان = و + زان, 'and/from that' (linking to what preceded). هدیه‌ها is the plural 'gifts'; شادمانی is a noun 'joy' serving as the direct object/topic of نمود, and نمود (past of نمودن) means 'showed' or 'displayed' — so the structure 'X شادمانی نمود' = 'he showed joy at X.'
segments.s587-g76: بران ('upon that' / 'thereon') refers back to the gifts/gesture. آفرین repeated is an exclamatory cry 'Afarin! Afarin!' (not the verb 'to create' here). برفزود is past (3rd sg.) meaning 'he increased / added' — i.e., he increased his praise or bestowed still more acclamations.
segments.s587-g77: وزان پس is a temporal/conjunctive sequence 'and from that, then' (linking to the previous action). فرستاده is 'the messenger/ambassador' and اندر نهفت is a verbal phrase 'hid inside/kept concealed' (اندر = 'in/within', نهفت = 'hidden/kept concealed' — here used as the action of concealing the envoy). The implied subject is the same ruler/person who acted earlier.
segments.s587-g78: ز بهرام چندی is a brief adverbial phrase 'a few (words) about Bahram' (ز = 'concerning / about', بهرام = name, چندی = 'some/a little'). به منذر بگفت is 'he said to Munḏir' (به = 'to', منذر = recipient, بگفت = past 'he said'). Word order places the topical phrase before the verb, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s587-g79: پس آن نامه برخواند: پس 'then'; آن نامه 'that letter'. برخواند here is the verb 'read aloud / recited' (past 3rd sg.). پیشش 'in his presence / before him' (پیش + enclitic), and دبیر 'the scribe/secretary' functions as the agent; the verb preceding the subject is a poetic inversion meaning 'the scribe read that letter before him.'
segments.s587-g80: رخ نامور گشت همچون زریر is a resultant descriptive clause: رخ 'face'; نامور 'renowned/famous'; گشت past of گشتن 'became' — so 'his face became renowned.' همچون زریر is a simile 'like Zarir.' The subject ('the scribe') is omitted here but understood from the previous clause.
segments.s587-g113: فرستاد is a past-tense verb 'he sent' (verb before object, typical in older Persian). زان = 'from that/those', placed before the noun it limits. ده سوار = 'ten horsemen' (ده numeral + سوار 'rider/horseman'), so the whole phrase means 'he sent ten horsemen from those Arabs.'
segments.s587-g114: A simple coordinate list of epithets/qualities linked by و 'and'. سخن‌گوی is a compound noun 'speak-er' (i.e. 'speaker' / eloquent one). بینادل is a rare/uncertain word (here left as an epithet). دوستدار is a noun/adjective 'one who is fond of' or 'a well-wisher/supporter'.
segments.s587-g115: رسیدند is past plural 'they arrived' (subject 'they' carried over from previous clause). نزدیک is an adverb/prepositional adverb 'near' and modifies رسیدند. بهرامشاه is the proper name 'Bahram-shah' as the place/person they approached.
segments.s587-g116: A coordinated nominal phrase (three items joined by و) in apposition to یا following بهرامشاه; each element is an epithet or name. The sequence functions as a list of descriptors/names attached to the preceding noun. Exact lexical senses of some items are uncertain in this context, so they are left unspecified where ambiguous.
segments.s587-g117: خردمند بهرام is a noun phrase with an adjective preceding the name: 'the wise Bahram.' زان = 'because of that / from that' (adverbial referring back). شاد شد is adjective 'شاد' + past auxiliary 'شد' meaning 'became happy'; this is a standard adjectival predicate construction ('became glad').
segments.s587-g118: همه دردها is the subject 'all the pains/sorrows' (دردها = plural). بر دلش is a prepositional phrase 'upon his heart' (دل + possessive clitic ش = 'his'). باد شد is a classical/modal phrase: باد is the optative/wish-like particle (used here in a poetic/idiomatic change-of-state expression) and شد is the past auxiliary 'became/was', so together they mark a resulting or chang­ing state affecting the heart.
segments.s587-g119: وزان پس is an adverbial sequence meaning 'and from that time / thereafter.' بـ (written as ب here) is the preposition/prefix 'to/unto' attached to the demonstrative or pronominal element that follows; دان in the given data appears as a morphological item but functions here as part of the phrase 'بدان پند' = 'to that counsel/advice' (i.e. 'following that advice'). شاه عرب is the noun phrase 'the Arab king' (noun + ethnic/epithet).
segments.s587-g120: پرستش بدی is a noun + modifying element meaning 'evil worship' (پرستش 'worship', بدی 'evil/bad' modifying it). کار او 'his work/occupation' places that activity as his daily business, and روز و شب 'day and night' is a time adverbial. The whole string says that evil worship became his constant occupation day and night.
segments.s587-g17: ازان پس (literally 'from that, then') is a formulaic temporal sequence meaning 'after that.' چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' آگاهی آمد is a simple past clause ('information/news came/arrived'); آمد is 3rd person singular past. به شاه places the recipient after the verb with the preposition به ('to the king').
segments.s587-g18: ز introduces the content/source of the news in an older/poetic sense 'concerning/about' (poetic equivalent of از). The coordinated nouns فرزند و نعمان تازی give who the news was about ('the son and Nuʿmān, the Arab'), and به راه ('on the road') is a locative phrase showing their condition. The epithet تازی (Arabic/Arab) follows the personal name as usual in Persian.
segments.s587-g19: بیامد is the past tense 'he came.' هم‌انگاه is an adverb 'immediately/at once.' نزد پدر is a prepositional phrase 'to his father's presence' (نزد used here as 'in the presence of/near'). Word order is verb + adverb + prepositional phrase, typical in narrative.
segments.s587-g20: چو = 'when' introduces a temporal clause. دیدش is the past verb 'saw' with the enclitic pronoun -ش = 'him' (i.e. 'saw him'); the clitic attaches to the verb. پدر را uses را as an object/topic marker (poetic word order places پدر after the verb). برآورد سر is a compound where برآورد is a past form (here 'raised') and سر is the object 'head' — together 'raised his head.'
segments.s587-g21: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the presence of/before.' کیی is an adjective/national epithet 'Kayanian' modifying تخت 'throne' (genitive/attributive relation: 'the Kayanian throne'). او is the pronoun 'he' placed in apposition, and سرفراز is an epithet 'proud / honored' describing him. Poetic order places modifiers and epithets after the noun.
segments.s587-g22: بیامد شتابان repeats the past verb 'he came' with the adverbial adjective شتابان 'hastily/swiftly.' و links the next clause. بردش is a past verb form with the enclitic -ش = 'him' (i.e. 'took/led him'); نماز is the direct object 'prayer' — together indicating he took/led him to prayer. The clitic marks the person affected.
segments.s587-g23: چو = 'when.' بهرام را is the object with را marking the proper name 'Bahram.' دید is the past 'saw/beheld.' بیدار (here predicative/adjectival) and شاه (the subject 'the king') follow in poetic order: the clause says that upon seeing Bahram the king was (or saw him) awake; syntactically the adjectives and nouns are arranged for emphasis.
segments.s587-g24: ب + دان here form the fused prepositional/conjunctive sense 'to/at that' (بدان) introducing what was seen/admired. فر is the technical poetic term 'farr' (royal/divine glory). The repeated آن before شاخ and گردگاه is a demonstrative used for poetic emphasis ('that horn and that neck'), and the coordination simply lists these aspects of Bahram's appearance.

sentences.s587-s33: It was not that my eye of hope was fixed on the king, that he should thus turn his gaze toward one inferior.
sentences.s587-s34: Neither is my son here, nor (is there) a servant, nor (is there) a lesser one, merry‑hearted at the gate.
sentences.s587-s35: He told Nu'mān what had been hidden concerning the king's bad ways and customs.
sentences.s587-s36: When Nu'mān went out from the king's gate, he came to the famed Munḏir.
sentences.s587-s53: Whenever you take dinars for a job, do not at all put on an air of weight toward the king.
sentences.s587-s54: For I have sent you many others as well, and many weighty (matters) for the kingship from every sort of thing.
sentences.s587-s55: Be a worshipper and be a praiser; be increasing in the work of worship.
sentences.s587-s56: You cannot secretly separate that bad disposition from the king of the world.
sentences.s587-s1: The father wished for Bahram — what a Bahram, the autocratic sun!
sentences.s587-s2: To Munḏir Bahram the Lion thus said: 'Although we may linger long in your presence.'
sentences.s587-s3: I rose to fulfill that very wish of my father; when I am secure I will rouse (them).
sentences.s587-s4: Munḏir set out when the task required it — a royal tribute from the city of Yemen.
sentences.s587-s21: For this deed the reward is with me; your spring belongs to Ahura Mazda.
sentences.s587-s22: I approved this counsel and his manner, for I see that his aim is toward wisdom.
sentences.s587-s23: Since you have tarried long at this court, father truly keeps his eye on the road.
sentences.s587-s24: From the treasury's dinars they gave five thousand, together with the king's robe.
sentences.s587-s5: From Arabian steeds to golden fittings — from whatever valuable things they took, they gained renown.
sentences.s587-s6: From Yemeni spoils and the sword of Yemen; whatever other mines it has are poor in ʿAden.
sentences.s587-s7: When Nu'mān, who was accompanying the king, was present, near him lay a gleaming helmet.
sentences.s587-s8: Thus they proceeded until they came to the city of Istakhr, where they arrived from the prince with pride.
sentences.s587-s49: I will send it — look: you have no heart for hardship; your scattered toil is not worth a treasure.
sentences.s587-s50: I have now sent a treasure of ten thousand dinars as an offering.
sentences.s587-s51: Where is the attendant who was your guide? Within the veil there was your heart-opener.
sentences.s587-s52: I have now sent (him/it) to your vicinity so that he may illumine your dark soul.
sentences.s587-s45: The heavens' judgment about the soul is this: with your opinion and his there is no secure refuge/foothold.
sentences.s587-s46: Some hearts the heavens keeps full of love; (and) some hearts full of hatred and faces full of warlike glare.
sentences.s587-s47: The sovereign of the world thus created the realm; that which so remained must be fastened (shut).
sentences.s587-s48: From now on, whatever may be useful to you — whether dinars or regal gems.
sentences.s587-s17: Night and day Bahram, in his father's presence, would always not lift his head from worship.
sentences.s587-s18: When Nu'mān, like a moon, appeared before the king, he wished that he return to the road.
sentences.s587-s19: He sent someone by night and had him summoned, then set him on the royal throne opposite him.
sentences.s587-s20: Munḏir said to him, 'I suffered greatly, because I raised the noble Bahram.'
sentences.s587-s13: Amazement remained at his deeds, at his lofty rank and refinement, and at his appearance.
sentences.s587-s14: He questioned him at length and entertained him, and set him a place near himself.
sentences.s587-s15: He chose for Nu'mān a place within the plaza, and allotted him one of Bahram's palaces, as was fitting.
sentences.s587-s16: He sent servants near him, like worshippers within his household.
sentences.s587-s25: At last, with silver and golden bridles, they took ten precious steeds and won renown.
sentences.s587-s26: Also (there were) comely caparisons and all manner of colors, scents, and every sort of thing.
sentences.s587-s27: From the treasury of the world‑ruler of Iran he took (them) and, one by one, entrusted them to Nuʿmān and Munḏir.
sentences.s587-s28: In joy, in the act of giving he opened wide and, according to each man’s rank, bestowed gifts on his companions.
sentences.s587-s29: The king wrote a letter to Munẓir, just as was fitting before the royal presence.
sentences.s587-s30: He relieved his son of his duties, for his son had become king of Yemen and an ally of his.
sentences.s587-s31: As a reward for this deed, he likewise granted me such a proud son.
sentences.s587-s32: Bahram Gur wrote a letter saying that my affairs here are ruined and in turmoil.
sentences.s587-s41: At the same time, he quickly wrote a reply; he wrote words full of sense and felicity.
sentences.s587-s42: He said thus, ‘O renowned chief, look — do not turn your head away from your father's path.’
sentences.s587-s43: Be pleasing to the king in good and ill; be a devotee and be wise.
sentences.s587-s44: Evils, by patience, pass away from guests; the head of a man must be one who has wisdom (a man ought to possess wisdom).
sentences.s587-s37: He gave him the king of the world's letter; Munḏir kissed it and set it upon his head.
sentences.s587-s38: He showed joy at those gifts; on that he cried 'Afarin! Afarin!' and added still more praise.
sentences.s587-s39: Then he hid the envoy away, and after a while he said a few words to Munḏir about Bahram.
sentences.s587-s40: Then the scribe read that letter before him; the scribe's face became renowned like Zarir's.
sentences.s587-s57: He sent ten horsemen from those Arabs, eloquent, 'بینادل', and well-disposed.
sentences.s587-s58: They reached near Bahram-shah — Aba Badreh, Barde, and Nik-khah.
sentences.s587-s59: The wise Bahram became glad because of that; all his sorrows came upon his heart.
sentences.s587-s60: And from then on, following that counsel, the Arab king made evil worship his occupation day and night.
sentences.s587-s9: After that, when news reached the king that his son and Nuʿmān the Arab were on the road.
sentences.s587-s10: He came at once to his father; when the father saw him, he raised his head.
sentences.s587-s11: He hastened into the presence of the Kayanian on his throne, proud; he came swiftly and took him to prayer.
sentences.s587-s12: When the king saw Bahram awake, he beheld that splendour — that crest and that noble bearing of the neck.
